currency.name: Somali shilling (SOS)
currency.rate: 1 USD ≈ 571 SOS (official) — USD dominates in practice
currency.note: The US dollar is the de facto currency for all significant transactions. The shilling is used for petty purchases only. Somaliland uses its own Somaliland shilling (SLSH). Cash-only economy in most of the country.

crypto.note: No legal framework. However, Somalia is actually a mobile payment pioneer — Hormuud's EVC Plus is one of Africa's most used mobile money systems. Crypto exists informally but no infrastructure.

article:
🧭 **Overview**
Somalia is a nation that has existed in a state of fragmentation since the collapse of Siad Barre's government in 1991. What followed was three decades of civil war, warlordism, famine, and one of the world's most complex humanitarian emergencies. The Federal Government of Somalia (FGS), based in Mogadishu, was re-established in 2012 with international support and has been gradually expanding control. Al-Shabaab, an al-Qaeda-affiliated jihadist group, still controls significant rural territory and carries out regular bombings. The country has Africa's longest mainland coastline (3,025km) and a strategic position at the Horn of Africa. Three zones operate semi-independently: the Federal Government (Mogadishu and the south), Puntland (northeast, autonomous), and Somaliland (northwest — self-declared independent since 1991, not internationally recognized, but the most stable and functional zone). Somalia's story is one of the most complex in the world: once written off as permanently failed, it is slowly, improbably rebuilding — but remains one of the most dangerous places on Earth.

👥 **People & vibe**
Somalis are remarkably ethnically homogeneous by African standards — one language, one religion (Sunni Islam, ~99%) — but deeply divided by clan (Dir, Darod, Hawiye, and Rahanweyn are the major groups, each subdivided into hundreds of sub-clans). Clan identity governs politics, business, security, and daily life entirely. Somalis are known for entrepreneurial intensity, fierce independence, a rich oral poetry tradition, and the massive diaspora ($2B+/year in remittances — more than foreign aid). The vibe in Mogadishu today is paradoxical: construction cranes and suicide bomb craters, new hotels and blast barriers. Somaliland's Hargeisa is calm, functional, and genuinely pleasant by comparison.

[[MAP]]

🌦️ **Climate & landscape**
Primarily hot and arid. Two rainy seasons: Gu (April–June, main rains) and Deyr (Oct–Nov, lighter). Temperatures: 28–40°C on the coast; interior more extreme. Northern highlands (Somaliland) are drier and cooler at altitude. Landscape includes Indian Ocean coastline, Sahelian scrubland, the Jubba and Shabelle river valleys (the only reliable agricultural zones), and northern highlands. Droughts are frequent and catastrophic — the 2011 famine killed 250,000+ people.

🏠 **Housing & settling in**
In Mogadishu, all international staff live in fortified compounds — the UN uses the heavily protected Halane base near the airport; INGOs have their own secured compounds. Movement between locations requires armored vehicles and security protocols for each trip. Compound rents: $2,500–6,000/month depending on security level. In Hargeisa (Somaliland), the situation is entirely different — normal neighborhoods, taxis, local restaurants, rents $400–900/month. Outside these cities, no expat infrastructure. Independent housing in Mogadishu without institutional backing is not an option.

💼 **Work & economy**
Key sectors: livestock exports ($500M+/year — camels, goats, cattle), diaspora remittances (~$2B/year, the actual economic backbone), telecoms (uniquely innovative mobile money), and humanitarian operations. Al-Shabaab also runs a parallel taxation economy estimated at $100M+/year. For foreigners: UN/AU Mission (AUSSOM), INGOs (IRC, NRC, Mercy Corps), diplomatic missions, or niche consulting. Diaspora returnees dominate the emerging private sector. Hardship-adjusted salaries: $5,000–10,000+/month for senior international staff.

🛂 **Visa & entry**
Visa on arrival at Mogadishu's Aden Adde Airport ($60 for most nationalities). Somaliland has its own entry process (separate stamp in Hargeisa — note that a Somaliland stamp can cause problems for subsequent Somalia entry). Travel outside Mogadishu requires UNDSS security clearance; most of southern Somalia is 'Phase 4–5' (off-limits for UN staff without special authorization). Armed escort required for all field missions.

🏥 **Healthcare**
Healthcare infrastructure was destroyed in the civil war and has barely recovered. Mogadishu has a handful of private clinics for basic care. All serious conditions require evacuation to Nairobi. Life expectancy: ~57 years. Malaria is endemic (coastal and river areas). Cholera outbreaks are regular. Polio has been largely eliminated through massive campaigns. Trauma care (blast/gunshot wounds) is paradoxically the most developed specialty. Emergency evacuation insurance is absolutely mandatory.

🚗 **Transport & mobility**
Mogadishu has paved roads but every movement requires a security assessment. Armored vehicles are standard for internationals. Aden Adde Airport connects to Nairobi (multiple airlines), Dubai, Addis Ababa, Istanbul, and regional cities. Domestic flights to Hargeisa, Garowe, Kismaayo, and Baidoa. In Hargeisa, normal taxis function fine. Rural Somalia: 4x4 only, with local clan knowledge essential for access.

🍽️ **Food note (national dish)**
The centerpiece is **Bariis iskukaris** — fragrant rice cooked with cumin, cardamom, and cinnamon, served with camel or goat meat. **Canjeero** (spongy sourdough flatbread, similar to Ethiopian injera) is the breakfast staple. **Camel milk** is culturally central and consumed fresh daily. **Sambuusa** (Somali samosas — fried triangles with spiced meat) are everywhere as street food. The cuisine reflects Arab, Indian, and East African influences from centuries of Indian Ocean trade.

🔎 **Bottom line**
Somalia is the ultimate paradox of modern Africa: a country the world once wrote off as permanently collapsed that is slowly, improbably rebuilding. Mogadishu has new hotels, a growing private sector, and a diaspora generation returning to invest and govern. Al-Shabaab still bombs it regularly. Somaliland, meanwhile, is an unrecognized state that functions better than most of its recognized neighbors. For expats: only engage with institutional support, master the clan architecture, and understand that the security environment is among the world's most complex. Those who go in with eyes open find a country of extraordinary resilience, entrepreneurial energy, and cultural depth. Most of the world has no idea what's actually happening here.

Expat Score — 2.0 / 10

national_dish.name: Bariis iskukaris / Canjeero / Camel milk
national_dish.note: Fragrant spiced rice with goat or camel, sourdough flatbread, and fresh camel milk. Indian Ocean trade (Arab, Indian, Persian) defines the cuisine.

lgbt_acceptance.note: Homosexuality carries up to 3 years in federal law; death penalty under al-Shabaab sharia in their territories. Completely taboo across all clan and religious lines. Not survivable openly under any circumstances.

personality.name: Hassan Sheikh Mohamud
personality.story: Elected president in 2012 and re-elected in 2022, Hassan Sheikh Mohamud represents something unusual in Somali politics: a civilian intellectual, not a warlord or military figure. A university rector and civil society leader, he has worked to extend federal authority and launched the first coordinated military offensive against al-Shabaab in a decade. Progress is real but fragile — he governs from behind blast walls, his authority dependent on African Union troops and clan coalition-building. The fact that Somalia now has a functioning electoral process, however imperfect, is itself remarkable given where the country was in 2000.

cards.title: ☠️ Al-Shabaab — the self-financing jihadist state
cards.text: Al-Shabaab (The Youth) emerged in 2006 and at its peak (2010–11) controlled most of southern Somalia. Pushed back by African Union forces, it shifted to insurgency and has proven extraordinarily durable. It still controls large rural areas, taxes farmers and businesses across southern Somalia (including in government cities through intimidation), and carries out regular complex attacks. It earns an estimated $100M/year — making it al-Qaeda's most financially self-sufficient affiliate globally. Defeating it militarily has proven impossible; the 2022–23 offensive made gains but hasn't broken it.

cards.title: 📱 Mobile money — innovation from collapse
cards.text: Somalia, a state with no functioning banking system for 20 years, became one of Africa's mobile money pioneers. Hormuud Telecom's EVC Plus launched in the early 2000s and became the primary financial infrastructure for millions. Today Somalia has some of the highest mobile money penetration in Africa. The complete absence of legacy banking paradoxically enabled innovation. Somali mobile money is studied by fintech developers worldwide as a model of leapfrog development. Diaspora remittances flow primarily through these mobile systems.

cards.title: 🏴 Somaliland — the world's most ignored success story
cards.text: Somaliland declared independence in 1991 after Siad Barre's forces massacred thousands in the north. It has since built a functioning democracy with multiple peaceful elections (including transfers of power between parties — rare in the region), its own currency, army, police, and passport. No country recognizes it. Hargeisa is safe, functional, and growing. The international community ignores it because recognizing Somaliland would complicate the 'one Somalia' fiction that underpins billions in aid architecture and African Union territorial integrity norms.

cards.title: 🌊 Piracy — the peak, the decline, and why it started
cards.text: Between 2008 and 2012, Somali pirates hijacked hundreds of ships in the Gulf of Aden, earning over $400M in ransoms and disrupting global shipping routes. An unprecedented multinational naval coalition (EU, NATO, US, China, India) crushed it by 2013. What's often omitted: Somali piracy emerged partly because foreign trawlers — including EU-flagged vessels — were illegally depleting Somali fish stocks for years while the state was too collapsed to stop them. The fishermen who became pirates had watched their livelihoods stolen first.

cards.title: 📜 The nation of poets — oral literature as politics
cards.text: Somalia is sometimes called 'a nation of poets.' The gabay (classical Somali poetry form) is the highest cultural expression — historical record, political manifesto, love poetry, and propaganda all transmitted through verse. Before Somali had a written script (standardized only in 1972), poetry was the primary means of transmitting history and ideas across a nomadic society. Political leaders used poetry to build alliances or declare war. The tradition persists: modern Somali presidents, generals, and diaspora figures still compose and recite gabay as a serious political act.

cards.title: 🐪 Camel culture — more than an animal
cards.text: Somalia is the world's largest exporter of live camels and one of the largest livestock exporters overall — $500M+/year to Gulf countries. But camels are far more than an export commodity. Bride price (mahar) is calculated in camels. Camel milk (nourishing, rich in vitamins C and B) is a daily dietary staple and believed to have medicinal properties. Classical Somali poetry frequently celebrates the camel. Clan disputes over camel ownership have historically triggered wars. The livestock trade survived the entire civil war intact — it required no state infrastructure, only clan networks.
